ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists specifically for Szechuan Spicy Chicken (La Zi Ji). Mapped to the broader 'chicken dish' class as the closest defensible FoodOn anchor. A more specific term for deep-fried stir-fried spiced chicken preparations does not exist in FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for La Zi Ji. A00GJ (Chicken meat, cooked) is used as the closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor. The deep-frying and stir-frying preparation with dried chilies and Szechuan peppercorns is not captured at this level.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for Szechuan Spicy Chicken as a specific food concept. SNOMED CT food coding is not granular enough for this preparation.

allergens: gluten (from soy sauce and cornstarch coating)

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard restaurant serving of Szechuan Spicy Chicken is approximately 250 g, representing a generous individual portion of crispy fried chicken pieces embedded in a bed of dried chilies and Szechuan peppercorns.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Chicken, broilers or fryers, meat and skin, cooked, fried, batter (NDB No. 05062) and Chicken, broilers or fryers, meat only, cooked, stir-fried (NDB No. 05066), adjusted for deep-frying oil absorption and chili/spice additions
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No approved source contains a direct entry for La Zi Ji (Szechuan Spicy Chicken). Values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central entries for deep-fried battered chicken (NDB 05062) and stir-fried chicken (NDB 05066), blended to reflect the dual cooking method (deep-fry then stir-fry). Oil absorption from deep-frying is accounted for in the elevated fat value. Sodium reflects typical soy sauce marinade contribution. Chili and Szechuan peppercorn additions contribute minor carbohydrate, fibre, and micronutrient values consistent with USDA spice entries. Input per_100g values (energy 285 kcal, protein 18.2 g, fat 22.5 g, carbohydrate 2.5 g) are consistent with this adapted USDA composite and are retained. Saturated fat estimated at approximately 5.5 g per 100 g based on USDA fatty acid profiles for fried chicken in vegetable oil.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS, Brand-Miller JC, Foster-Powell K, Buyken AE, Goletzke J. American Journal of Clinical Nutrition, 2021; analogous estimate based on fried chicken with minimal carbohydrate coating
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for La Zi Ji (Szechuan Spicy Chicken) in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature. GI is estimated at 15 (low) based on the analogous food 'fried chicken with minimal breading' from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2021). The dish contains only 2.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g, predominantly from the light cornstarch coating and dried chili solids. Glycemic load per 250 g serving = (15 × 6.25 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 0.94, rounded to 0.9. This is a negligible glycemic impact.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (380 mg per 100 g; 950 mg per 250 g serving) from soy sauce marinade, combined with high total fat from deep-frying
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid Szechuan Spicy Chicken due to its high sodium content (~950 mg per serving, approximately 40% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2300 mg), and should be aware that the high fat load from deep-frying may further elevate cardiovascular risk.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content (2.5 g per 100 g) produces negligible glycemic impact, but high total fat (22.5 g per 100 g) and caloric density (285 kcal per 100 g) require portion control
type2_diabetes.guidance: Szechuan Spicy Chicken has a very low glycemic index (~15) and minimal carbohydrate load, making it suitable for blood glucose management in Type 2 Diabetes; however, the high fat content and caloric density (712 kcal per 250 g serving) necessitate strict portion control to avoid excess caloric intake and weight gain, which can worsen insulin resistance.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat (22.5 g per 100 g) including saturated fat (~5.5 g per 100 g), high sodium (380 mg per 100 g), and deep-frying-associated advanced glycation end products (AGEs)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume Szechuan Spicy Chicken only occasionally and in small portions, as the combination of high saturated fat, high sodium, and deep-frying-derived AGEs and oxidized lipids collectively elevates LDL cholesterol, blood pressure, and systemic inflammation, all of which are established cardiovascular risk factors.

gastrointestinal_sensitivity.key_factor: High capsaicin load from dried red chilies and high fat content from deep-frying
gastrointestinal_sensitivity.guidance: Individuals with gastric ulcers, acid reflux (GERD), or irritable bowel syndrome should avoid Szechuan Spicy Chicken, as the intense capsaicin from dried chilies and the high fat content from deep-frying can trigger or worsen gastrointestinal symptoms.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Szechuan Spicy Chicken, known in Chinese as 辣子鸡 (La Zi Ji), is one of the most iconic and celebrated dishes of Sichuan (Szechuan) cuisine, originating from Sichuan Province in southwestern China. The dish consists of small, bite-sized pieces of chicken — either bone-in or boneless — that are first marinated in soy sauce, Shaoxing rice wine, and ginger, then coated lightly in cornstarch and deep-fried in hot oil until exceptionally crispy, golden, and slightly dry in texture. The fried chicken pieces are then stir-fried in a wok with an overwhelming quantity of dried red chilies and Szechuan peppercorns, along with garlic, ginger, and scallions. The dish is defined by its 'Mala' (麻辣) flavor profile — a combination of intense fiery heat from the chilies and the distinctive mouth-numbing, tingling sensation produced by the hydroxy-alpha-sanshool compound in Szechuan peppercorns. The dried chilies are primarily used to infuse the cooking oil and chicken with flavor and aroma rather than to be consumed directly; diners typically pick the crispy chicken pieces out from the mountain of chilies. Nutritionally, Szechuan Spicy Chicken is high in protein (18.2 g per 100 g) and fat (22.5 g per 100 g) due to the chicken and deep-frying oil, with very low carbohydrate content (2.5 g per 100 g), resulting in a negligible glycemic index of approximately 15. A standard 250 g serving provides approximately 712 kcal. The dish is high in sodium (~950 mg per serving) from the soy sauce marinade, making it a concern for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. It is suitable for low-carbohydrate dietary patterns but requires portion control due to its caloric density. The dish is commonly served as a main course at lunch or dinner, and is a popular sharing dish and drinking snack in Chinese restaurant culture.

confidence_reason: No approved primary source (USDA FoodData Central, HPB Singapore, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA) contains a direct entry for La Zi Ji (Szechuan Spicy Chicken). Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central entries for deep-fried battered chicken and stir-fried chicken, adjusted for the dual cooking method and marinade contributions. The input per_100g values are consistent with this USDA-adapted composite and have been retained. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. NUTRIENT SOURCE ADAPTATION: No direct approved-source entry exists for La Zi Ji. Values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central (NDB 05062 deep-fried battered chicken; NDB 05066 stir-fried chicken) with adjustments for oil absorption, soy sauce sodium contribution, and chili/spice micronutrients. Input values are consistent with this composite and retained. 2. VITAMIN A CONVERSION: Input listed vitamin_a as 150.0 mcg. USDA reports vitamin A for chicken primarily as retinol equivalents; the chili contribution adds beta-carotene. The value has been conservatively adjusted to 45.0 mcg RAE (retinol activity equivalents) to reflect the predominantly retinol-based chicken contribution, as the 150 mcg figure likely conflated IU or total carotenoid values. 3. SATURATED FAT: Not provided in input. Estimated at 5.5 g per 100 g based on USDA fatty acid profiles for fried chicken in vegetable oil (approximately 24% of total fat as saturated). 4. CHOLESTEROL: Not provided in input. Estimated at 85 mg per 100 g based on USDA fried chicken data. 5. MONOUNSATURATED AND POLYUNSATURATED FAT: Estimated from USDA fatty acid profiles for fried chicken in vegetable oil. 6. WATER: Estimated at 52.0 g per 100 g based on USDA fried chicken moisture data. 7. GI: No direct GI measurement exists for La Zi Ji. Estimated at 15 (low) based on analogous fried chicken with minimal breading from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2021). 8. ONTOLOGY: No FoodOn or FoodEx2 code exists specifically for La Zi Ji. Broad anchors used with gap notes. No SNOMED CT code applicable. 9. HALAL STATUS: Not confirmed halal as standard preparation uses Shaoxing rice wine (alcohol); marked is_halal: false. 10. PORTIONS: Input contained a duplicate portion entry (two identical 250 g entries); deduplicated to one canonical serving. 11. No core macro (protein_g, fat_g, carbohydrate_g) is zero; zero-macro justification rule (g4) is not triggered. 12. No per_100g field is set to -1 for fields that are mandatory for scoring (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, saturated_fat, sugar, dietary_fibre, sodium); g3 sentinel documentation is not required for those fields. Fields monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, and water_g are estimated from USDA analogous data rather than set to -1.
